mission.title: Install and Configure MongoDB Community Operator
mission.description: The MongoDB Community Operator simplifies the deployment and management of MongoDB databases on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users who need a straightforward way to run MongoDB in a Kubernetes environment without the complexities of enterprise features. However, it is deprecated and users are encouraged to migrate to the new MongoDB Kubernetes Operator.

steps.title: Add the MongoDB Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MongoDB Community Operator Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add mongodb https://mongodb.github.io/mongodb-kubernetes-operator

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the MongoDB Helm repository.
> Updates the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts.

steps.title: Install the MongoDB Community Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MongoDB Community Operator using Helm.

```bash

helm install mongodb-community-operator mongodb/mongodb-kubernetes-operator --version 0.13.0 --namespace mongodb --create-namespace

```

> Installs the operator in a dedicated namespace.

steps.title: Create a MongoDB custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource definition (CRD) for MongoDB to deploy an instance.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mongodb.com/v1
kind: MongoDB
metadata:
  name: my-mongo
  namespace: mongodb
spec:
  members: 3
  type: ReplicaSet
  version: 5.0.3
  podSpec:
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
EOF

```

> This command creates a MongoDB instance with a replica set.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mongodb.com/v1
kind: MongoDB
metadata:
  name: my-mongo
  namespace: mongodb
spec:
  members: 3
  type: ReplicaSet
  version: 5.0.3
  podSpec:
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
EOF

steps.title: Verify the MongoDB deployment
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the MongoDB deployment to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb

kubectl get pods -n mongodb

```

> This command lists all MongoDB instances in the 'mongodb' namespace.
> This command lists all pods to verify that MongoDB pods are running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MongoDB Community Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MongoDB Community Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all MongoDB instances and data associated with them.

```bash

helm uninstall mongodb-community-operator --namespace mongodb

kubectl delete namespace mongodb

```

> Uninstalls the operator from the specified namespace.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MongoDB Community Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your MongoDB data. This step will upgrade the operator to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb -o yaml > mongodb-backup.yaml

helm upgrade mongodb-community-operator mongodb/mongodb-kubernetes-operator --version 0.13.0 --namespace mongodb

kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb

```

> Backup the current MongoDB custom resources.
> Upgrades the operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the MongoDB instances are still running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb -o yaml > mongodb-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create MongoDB resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the CRD is not installed correctly or if there are validation errors in the spec.

**Fix:**
Check the CRD installation with 'kubectl get crd' and validate your MongoDB spec.

troubleshooting.title: Pods are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen due to insufficient resources or misconfiguration in the MongoDB spec.

**Fix:**
Inspect the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n mongodb' and adjust resource requests/limits.

troubleshooting.title: MongoDB instance not starting
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This could be due to a misconfigured custom resource or issues with the underlying Kubernetes nodes.

**Fix:**
Check the status of the nodes with 'kubectl get nodes' and review the MongoDB CR for errors.

troubleshooting.title: Helm install fails with 'chart not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Helm repository has not been added or updated correctly.

**Fix:**
Run 'helm repo add mongodb https://mongodb.github.io/mongodb-kubernetes-operator' and 'helm repo update'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for MongoDB 5.0, improved resource management.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations noted in this version.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.11, ensure to validate your MongoDB CRD specifications.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb' showing your MongoDB instance with a status of 'Ready'.

kind: MongoDB
metadata:

  version: 5.0.3
  podSpec:
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
